assessments.task_description:
Overview

The purpose of this pitch is to seek (hypothetical) funding to deliver a program, activity, or event which responds to poverty or social disadvantage experienced by individuals or families in Australia.

More detailed information about how to approach the pitch is provided below. The marking rubric is available in the Assessment 1 folder on Blackboard.

Getting started

1.     Identify a social issue related to one of the topics we discuss in class.

2.     Pretend that you work for a charity or non-profit organisation that works in the area of addressing social disadvantage. This organisation can be real or imagined, but it must align with your chosen issue.

3.     Come up with a program, activity, or event designed to help your organisation address your identified issue. You can request up to $500,000 (hypothetical dollars) to deliver your program, activity, or event over a 12-month period.

4.     Write a persuasive pitch to secure funding to deliver your idea. 


AI Use: This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which AI may provide limited support and guidance. A failure to reference AI use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct Policy. Students may be required to complete an AI usage declaration and submit it with their assessment. Additional resources are available through UQ AI Student Hub.
assessments.submission_guidelines: This assessment item must be submitted via Turnitin on Blackboard Ultra.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
Overview

Based on the assumption that your proposed program, activity, or event from Assignment 1 was successfully funded and carried out, the purpose of this policy brief is to provide a concise and informative overview of what happened. This policy brief can be targeted either towards a government department with a vested interest in solving the problem you identified, or towards the organisation that provided the initial funding.

See the marking rubric in the Assessment 2 folder on Blackboard for detailed information on how we will mark your policy brief.

Getting started

1.     Think about how your program, activity or event might have played out in practice.

2.     Identify a whether your program, activity or event was able to achieve its core aim, as set out in your funding pitch.

3.     Identify some successes and challenges you encountered along the way.

4.     Draw on existing evidence from similar programs, activities or events to substantiate why you might have encountered these successes and challenges.

5.     Make recommendations based on your findings.


AI Use: This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which AI may provide limited support and guidance. A failure to reference AI use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct Policy. Students may be required to complete an AI usage declaration and submit it with their assessment. Additional resources are available through UQ AI Student Hub.
assessments.submission_guidelines: This assessment item must be submitted via Turnitin on Blackboard Ultra.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
Overview

For our in-class debate, you will be assigned to a team (approx. 4-5 people). Each team will debate one other team (this is a ‘pairing’). Each paring will be provided a broad topic and several associated readings. You will have several weeks to engage with these readings, learn about your topic, and confer with your team.

On the day of the debate, you will be given your specific motion (proposition) at the beginning of class, which will be related to your broad topic and associated readings, and advised whether your team will be arguing in the affirmative or negative. Your team will then have 30 minutes to discuss and plan your arguments. You will not be able to access technology during this time, but you may bring in 1 page of handwritten notes. You will also be given paper that you can use to write notes during the planning time, and you can have these notes with you when you present.

We will then move straight into the debate. During the debate, each team member will have 2 minutes to speak. Your marks will be assigned based on your individual performance, not the performance of your team as a whole.

See the marking rubric in the Assessment 3 folder on Blackboard for detailed information on how we will mark your debate. Please note the debate will be audio recorded through the classroom's Echo360 platform.

Getting started

1.     Read the provided associated readings.

2.     Identify and read other journal articles or academic sources that are related to your broad topic (this will expose you to diverse ways of thinking about your topic).

3.     Meet with other members of your team to discuss your learnings and thoughts about your topics. Discuss different ways of thinking about the problem. 


AI Use: This assessment task will be completed in-person. The use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct Policy.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You must attend class on the due date to complete your assessment in person.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.task_description:
You will receive 1% for each workshop you participate and actively engage in, up to a possible total of 10%. There will be opportunities to participate verbally during class, or through the submission of a hand-written reflection at the end of class.

You must therefore participate and engage in 10 of the following 12 workshops to receive the full 10%. We will calculate your grade based on your best 10 weeks.

·        Week 1 – Introduction and the social construction of problems

·        Week 2 – Poverty in Australia and what we do about it

·        Week 4 – How to write a funding pitch

·        Week 5 – The Park Hotel

·        Week 6 – Brisbane Common Ground

·        Week 7 – How to write a policy brief

·        Week 8 – Agency and responding to injustice

·        Week 9 – Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander families

·        Week 10 – How to approach a debate

·        Week 11 – Domestic and family violence

·        Week 12 – In-class debate

·        Week 13 – Bringing it together 

Please note there are no participation points associated with the public holiday in Week 3.


AI Use: This assessment task will be completed in-person. The use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct Policy.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You must be in class to receive your participation and in-class engagement marks. You may either express your contributions verbally during class, or you will be given time at the end of each class to hand-write a short reflection on what you have learned. This hand-written reflection must be handed into one of the course coordinators before you leave the class, must be legible, and must include your name and student number.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

learning_activities.topic:
Introduction and the social construction of problems

Introduction to the vision of SOSC3600. How does the way we think about policy problems influence the policies we design to address them? The bridge between students' employment aspirations and SOSC3600.


Learning outcomes: L01, L02
learning_activities.topic_title: Introduction and the social construction of problems
learning_activities.topic_description: Introduction and the social construction of problems Introduction to the vision of SOSC3600. How does the way we think about policy problems influence the policies we design to address them? The bridge between students' employment aspirations and SOSC3600.

learning_activities.topic:
Poverty in Australia and what we do about it

This workshop will focus on the three broad areas of (1) poverty, (2) the welfare state, and (3) charities and NGOs responding to poverty.


Learning outcomes: L01, L02
learning_activities.topic_title: Poverty in Australia and what we do about it
learning_activities.topic_description: Poverty in Australia and what we do about it This workshop will focus on the three broad areas of (1) poverty, (2) the welfare state, and (3) charities and NGOs responding to poverty.

learning_activities.topic:
How to write a funding pitch

This week is a seminar dedicated to the science and art of writing a funding pitch. This is a critical discussion for the first assessment.


Learning outcomes: L03, L04, L05
learning_activities.topic_description: How to write a funding pitch This week is a seminar dedicated to the science and art of writing a funding pitch. This is a critical discussion for the first assessment.

learning_activities.topic:
The Park Hotel

This week we go off campus and examine some recent efforts in Brisbane to meet the needs of people who are excluded from society.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_description: The Park Hotel This week we go off campus and examine some recent efforts in Brisbane to meet the needs of people who are excluded from society.

learning_activities.topic:
Brisbane Common Ground

This week we go off campus and take a first-hand look at some of the ways that Brisbane responds to poverty, including how it tries to end poverty by providing permanent supportive housing.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_description: Brisbane Common Ground This week we go off campus and take a first-hand look at some of the ways that Brisbane responds to poverty, including how it tries to end poverty by providing permanent supportive housing.

learning_activities.topic:
How to write a policy brief

Writing a policy brief is critical to convey knowledge to ensure that the knowledge is used. The seminar takes the class through the entire process of writing a policy brief, which is critical for Assessment 2.

Learning outcomes: L01, L03, L04, L05
learning_activities.topic_description: How to write a policy brief Writing a policy brief is critical to convey knowledge to ensure that the knowledge is used. The seminar takes the class through the entire process of writing a policy brief, which is critical for Assessment 2.

learning_activities.topic:
Agency and responding to injustice

This week we hear from Micah Projects CEO Karyn Walsh AM about how Micah Projects was founded by a small group of people united by a shared belief that they could solve the injustices they saw in society.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L05
learning_activities.topic_description: Agency and responding to injustice This week we hear from Micah Projects CEO Karyn Walsh AM about how Micah Projects was founded by a small group of people united by a shared belief that they could solve the injustices they saw in society.

learning_activities.topic:
Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander families

Family and kin relationships are central to Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples social and cultural life. As the course has shown to this point, Indigenous families are disadvantaged in many domains of Australian society. On the other hand, understanding and highlighting the strengths of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people is critical to realise not only positive family life, but true reconciliation in Australia.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02
learning_activities.topic_description: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander families Family and kin relationships are central to Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples social and cultural life. As the course has shown to this point, Indigenous families are disadvantaged in many domains of Australian society. On the other hand, understanding and highlighting the strengths of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people is critical to realise not only positive family life, but true reconciliation in Australia.

learning_activities.topic:
How to approach your debate

Debating is an important way to learn to understand other perspectives. The seminar takes the class through the process of debate, which is critical for Assessment 3.

Learning outcomes: L03, L04
learning_activities.topic_description: How to approach your debate Debating is an important way to learn to understand other perspectives. The seminar takes the class through the process of debate, which is critical for Assessment 3.

learning_activities.topic:
Domestic and family violence

Domestic and Family Violence (DFV) is a pervasive and deeply entrenched social issue in Australia. By this week, we will have heard countless references to DFV during our class discussions due to its inextricable interconnectedness to problems such as homelessness, child protection, and family dynamics. Indeed, DFV is now recognised as wicked social problem.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02

learning_activities.topic:
Bringing it together

It's been a big semester. Fast paced and many ideas and examples introduced, observed, and discussed. In this final session, we will critically reflect upon SOSC3600. We will ask what we have learnt, what has surprised and challenged us, and what is useful for us to take beyond.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L05
